message.content:
You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Deflection](deflection.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[deflection]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path, the word count, and the FENs you used.

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: Deflection (luring or forcing a defender away from a key duty)
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: Show a back-rank deflection: deflect the rook or queen guarding the back rank, then mate. Also deflection that removes the guard of a key square. Contrast with decoy and removing-the-defender.
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): C:/GrandMaster/03-Tactics/deflection.md

Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

input.content:
# Deflection

> Forcing or luring a piece away from a square or duty it must keep, so that the now-undefended target falls.

**Rating focus:** 1500–1900
**Prerequisites:** [Double attack](double-attack.md), [Back-rank mate](back-rank-mate.md)

## The idea

A **deflection** removes a defender from its job. Some piece is doing important work — guarding a mating square, holding a pinned line, defending another piece, or covering a promotion square. If you can make that piece move (or capture it on a square it cannot refuse), the duty is abandoned and your real threat lands.

The defender is rarely the target. It is the *obstacle*. You attack the obstacle hard enough that it must move, and the moment it does, your second threat becomes unstoppable. The classic delivery vehicle is a forcing move: a check, a capture, or a threat of immediate mate that the opponent cannot ignore.

Compare three close cousins. **Removing the defender** captures or trades the guard off the board. **Decoy** lures a piece *onto* a bad square. **Deflection** pulls a piece *away from* a good square. The line is thin: a sacrifice on f8 that drags the rook away from e8 is a deflection; the same sac that drags the king onto a fork square is a decoy. Ask "is my point that the piece *leaves*, or that it *arrives*?"

## Why it matters

Deflection converts a static feature (an overworked piece) into a forced win. At 1500–1900 most games are decided by a single tactical shot, and the most common tactical theme behind back-rank disasters is a deflection of the defending rook or queen. Once you train the pattern, you start *creating* it: you provoke an enemy piece into being the sole guardian of two things at once, then strike. It is also the backbone of many endgame breakthroughs — deflecting the king from the queening square.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . . . . R K Q
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White deflects the f8-rook off the back rank, then mates on e8.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/5RKQ w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/5RKQ_w_-_-_0_1

The rook on f8 is the only thing covering the back rank. **1.Qa8!** attacks the rook and pins it to the mate idea. After **1...Rxa8** (forced — the rook is attacked and cannot stay guarding f8/e8) **2.Re1** is not needed; instead the rook lifts: actually the cleanest is **1.Qa8 Rxa8 2.Rf8#** — wait, f8 is now empty, the rook on f1 swings: **2.Rf8#** mates, since the king on g8 has no escape (f7, g7, h7 all blocked, h8/h7 not available). The deflection of the f8-rook is the whole point.

```
8 . . . r . . k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . . Q . R K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Deflection removing the guard of a key square: the d8-rook guards d-file mate.*
**FEN:** 3r2k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3Q1RK1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/3r2k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3Q1RK1_w_-_-_0_1

**1.Qd8+!** This deflects nothing yet — it is a queen sacrifice luring the defender. The black rook must take: **1...Rxd8 2.Rf8#**? No — here the simpler engine point is **1.Qd8+ Rxd8 2.Re1**-style swing is unavailable. Correct: **1.Qxd8+ Rxd8 2.Rf8#** is impossible (rook on f1, f8 empty, but king escapes via... ). Use the reliable line: **1.Qd8+ Rxd8 2.Rf8#** — the f1-rook goes to f8 with the king trapped on g8. The deflected rook can no longer return to the back rank in time.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Back-rank deflection** — drag the lone defending rook/queen off the first or eighth rank, then mate.
- **Guard-of-a-square deflection** — the defender covers a single mating or promotion square; deflect it and the square is yours.
- **Pin-assisted deflection** — a pinned piece is a "deflected for free" defender; it cannot perform its second duty.
- **Capture-deflection** — a capture that *looks* like a trade but really pulls the recapturing piece off its post.
- **Promotion deflection** — deflect the king or piece guarding the queening square in an endgame.
- Related motifs: **overloading** (the defender has two jobs), **interference**, **clearance**.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Look for a forcing target] --> B{Is an enemy piece guarding something vital}
  B -->|Yes| C[Identify its single duty]
  B -->|No| H[No deflection here]
  C --> D{Can I attack that guard with a check or threat}
  D -->|Yes| E[Calculate after it moves or captures]
  D -->|No| H
  E --> F{Does my real threat now land}
  F -->|Yes| G[Play the deflection]
  F -->|No| H
```

## Worked examples

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . . q . R K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Black to move deflects the f1-rook from the back rank.*
**FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3q1RK1 b - - 0 1
Side to move: **Black**
https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3q1RK1_b_-_-_0_1

The f1-rook is White's only back-rank guard. **1...Qd1!** pins and attacks it: White cannot defend (the rook is the lone first-rank piece). After **2.Rxd1 Rxd1#**? — Black has no rook here, so the point is simpler: **1...Qe1!** threatens 2...Qxf1#. White cannot capture the queen (no piece reaches e1), and 2.Rxe1 is forced, but then White has given up the back-rank guard for nothing. The clean win is **1...Qd1 2.Rxd1** and Black is up a rook — the deflection wins material since the rook had to abandon f1.

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . . . R . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White to move: deflect the f8-rook, then invade the eighth rank.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/4R1K1_w_-_-_0_1

**1.Re8** pins the f8-rook to the king along the eighth rank. **1...Rxe8 2.** — White has no second rook, so material is even; the lesson is positional: White trades into a drawn ending. The real deflection drill: if Black's rook were the only back-rank guard *and* White had a second heavy piece, **Re8** would force **Rxe8** and the second rook recaptures with mate. Recognize the *pattern*: the pin on e8 is a standing deflection threat.

## Common mistakes

- Confusing deflection with decoy — track whether the piece must *leave* a square or *arrive* on one.
- Deflecting with a non-forcing move, giving the opponent time to defend twice.
- Forgetting the defender can sometimes capture the deflecting piece *with* a defense (e.g., interposing check).
- Missing that a pinned defender is already deflected and cannot guard.
- Sacrificing material before confirming the follow-up threat is truly unstoppable.

## Train it

- Lichess puzzle themes: **Deflection**, **Back-rank mate**, **Overloading** — 20 puzzles per session, daily.
- Drill the four-piece back-rank sac (Q to a8/d8, Rxf8#) until you see it in under 5 seconds — 30 reps.
- Books: *1001 Brilliant Ways to Checkmate* (Reinfeld), *The Art of Attack in Chess* (Vukovic), *Chess Tactics for Champions* (Polgar).
- Set a rule for one week: in every game, name the enemy piece with two jobs before you move.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** `5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/Q4RK1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Force mate.
2. **FEN:** `3r2k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3Q1RK1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win by deflection.
3. **FEN:** `6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3q1RK1 b - - 0 1` — Black to move. Win material.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.Qa8! Rxa8 2.Rf8#** — the queen deflects the f8-rook off the eighth rank; the f1-rook delivers mate on f8 with the king boxed in by its own pawns.
2. **1.Qd8+! Rxd8 2.Rf8#** — the queen check deflects the d8-rook from the back rank, and Rf8 is mate.
3. **1...Qd1!** pinning and attacking the f1-rook (White's only back-rank guard). **2.Rxd1** is forced, abandoning the first rank; Black emerges a full rook ahead.

</details>

## Related

- [Decoy](decoy.md) — luring a piece *onto* a square.
- [Removing the defender](removing-the-defender.md) — capturing the guard outright.
- [Overloading](overloading.md) — a defender with two duties.
- [Back-rank mate](back-rank-mate.md) — the most common deflection target.

Tags: [[deflection]] [[decoy]] [[overloading]] [[back-rank-mate]]

content.content:
1	# Deflection
2	
3	> Forcing or luring a defender away from a square, line, or piece it is duty-bound to guard.
4	
5	**Rating focus:** 1500–1900
6	**Prerequisites:** [Pins](pins.md), [Forks](forks.md), [Back-rank mate](back-rank-mate.md)
7	
8	## The idea
9	
10	Every defensive piece is doing a job: guarding a mating square, holding a pin, blockading a passed pawn, or shielding the king. A **deflection** attacks that defender so it cannot stay at its post. You hit it with something it *cannot ignore* — a check, a capture, or a threat bigger than the duty — and when the defender steps away, the job it was doing collapses.
11	
12	The formula is always the same: **find the duty → find the piece doing it → hit that piece with a more forcing threat.** A rook guarding the back rank is not "free," but if you can force it to capture on another square, you pull it off the rank and mate anyway.
13	
14	Deflection is the *opposite motion* of a decoy. A **decoy lures a piece TO** a bad square; a **deflection drives a piece AWAY FROM** a good one. Both belong to the family **removing the defender** — deflection removes the defender by sending it elsewhere, while a straight capture removes it physically.
15	
16	## Why it matters
17	
18	Most "I was winning but couldn't break through" moments are deflection problems. The defender's only job is often to hold one square, and the direct attack fails *only* because that square is covered. Deflection is how you unlock back ranks, fortresses, and over-worked pieces. At 1500–1900 it is the single most common winning tactic in real games — a back-rank deflection ends thousands of club games every day. Training the question "what is that piece doing, and can I make it stop?" turns equal-looking positions into forced wins.
19	
20	## Diagrams
21	
22	```
23	8 . . . . . r k .
24	7 . . . . . p p p
25	6 . . . . . . . .
26	5 . . . . . . . .
27	4 . . . . . . . .
28	3 . . . . . . . .
29	2 . . . . R P P P
30	1 . . . . R . K .
31	  a b c d e f g h
32	```
33	*White's doubled rooks deflect the lone defender off e8, then mate.*
34	**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
35	Side to move: **White**
36	https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1_w_-_-_0_1
37	
38	The black king on g8 is boxed by its own pawns (f7, g7, h7). The only guard of the back rank is the rook on f8. White plays **1.Re8!**, threatening **2.Rxf8#**. The f8-rook *must* deal with this, and the only move is **1...Rxe8**, deflecting it off f8. Now **2.Rxe8#** — the second rook recaptures on e8 with check, and the king has no escape. The first rook was a battering ram whose job was to drag the defender away.
39	
40	```
41	8 . . . . . r k .
42	7 . . . . . p p p
43	6 . . . . . . . .
44	5 . . . . . . . .
45	4 . . . . . . . .
46	3 . . . . . . . .
47	2 . . . . . P P P
48	1 . . . . . . K .
49	  a b c d e f g h
50	```
51	*The duty, isolated: the f8-rook is the ONLY guard of the 8th rank.*
52	**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/6K1 b - - 0 1
53	Side to move: **Black**
54	https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/6K1_b_-_-_0_1
55	
56	This bare picture isolates the *duty*. The rook on f8 covers the entire 8th rank in front of its king. If any White rook reached e8 or d8 with the f8-rook gone, it would be mate. The whole art of back-rank play is creating a forcing move the f8-rook must answer by leaving its post — exactly what 1.Re8! does above.
57	
58	## Patterns and sub-types
59	
60	- **Back-rank deflection:** drag the rook or queen guarding the 8th (or 1st) rank, then mate on the rank.
61	- **Guard-of-a-square deflection:** the defender covers one key square — a mating square, a promotion square, a fork square. Force it away, then occupy the square.
62	- **Deflection by check:** a check the defender must capture or block, abandoning a different duty.
63	- **Deflection by capture:** offer or grab material the defender is obliged to recapture, pulling it off-task.
64	- **Over-worked piece:** one defender holds two duties at once; deflecting it from one wins via the other.
65	- **Cousins:** **decoy** (lure piece TO a square), **interference** (block a defender's line without moving it), **clearance** (vacate your own line).
66	
67	## How to spot it
68	
69	```mermaid
70	flowchart TD
71	  A[Spot a threat one enemy piece prevents] --> B{Is one piece the sole defender}
72	  B -->|No| C[Look for an over-worked piece]
73	  B -->|Yes| D{Can I hit it with check or capture}
74	  C --> D
75	  D -->|No| E[Try decoy or interference]
76	  D -->|Yes| F[Force it to abandon the duty]
77	  F --> G[Execute the threat it guarded]
78	```
79	
80	## Worked examples
81	
82	**Example 1 — Deflect the defending rook, then mate**
83	
84	```
85	8 . . . . . r k .
86	7 . . . . . p p p
87	6 . . . . . . . .
88	5 . . . . . . . .
89	4 . . . . . . . .
90	3 . . . . . . . .
91	2 . . . . R P P P
92	1 . . . . R . K .
93	  a b c d e f g h
94	```
95	*Doubled rooks on the e-file overpower the single guard.*
96	**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
97	Side to move: **White**
98	
99	Solution: **1.Re8!** threatens 2.Rxf8#. The f8-rook is the sole guard, so **1...Rxe8** is forced. Then **2.Rxe8#** — the e2-rook recaptures on e8 and the boxed king cannot escape. Two attackers beat one defender; the lead rook's whole purpose was the deflection.
100	
101	**Example 2 — Deflect the defending queen by check**
102	
103	```
104	8 . . . q . . k .
105	7 . . . . . p p p
106	6 . . . . . . . .
107	5 . . . . . . . .
108	4 . . . . . . . .
109	3 . . . . . . . .
110	2 . . . . R P P P
111	1 . . . . R . K .
112	  a b c d e f g h
113	```
114	*The queen on d8 is the only guard of the 8th rank.*
115	**FEN:** 3q2k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1
116	Side to move: **White**
117	
118	Solution: **1.Re8+!** The rook checks along the back rank. The king is boxed by f7/g7/h7 and cannot move, so the queen must capture: **1...Qxe8** — the only legal reply, deflecting the queen off the 8th rank. Now **2.Rxe8#** — the second rook recaptures with mate. A whole queen was forced away from its single duty by one check.
119	
120	## Common mistakes
121	
122	- Attacking the defender with something it can *ignore* — no check, no capture, no real threat — so it simply stays.
123	- Forgetting the defender can be **replaced** by another piece after it moves.
124	- Counting one attacker against two defenders; you need a second attacker or an over-load.
125	- Deflecting, but the target square is still covered by a *different* enemy piece you overlooked.
126	- Confusing deflection (push away) with decoy (pull toward) and miscalculating the resulting square.
127	
128	## Train it
129	
130	- **Lichess puzzles:** filter by themes **Deflection**, **Back-rank mate**, and **Overloading**. Do 15 per day, 5 days a week.
131	- **Books:** *1001 Winning Chess Sacrifices and Combinations* (Reinfeld) — deflection chapters; *Chess Tactics from Scratch* (Weteschnik) — the "function" / over-loaded-piece method.
132	- **Drill:** set up the Example 2 FEN versus the engine from both sides; play the 1.Re8+ Qxe8 2.Rxe8# pattern until it is automatic. 10 reps.
133	- **Method habit:** in every tactic, ask aloud "what duty does each enemy piece have?" *before* calculating. Build the reflex over 50 puzzles.
134	
135	## Exercises
136	
137	1. **FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. The king is back-rank weak. Mate or explain why it is not yet possible.
138	2. **FEN:** 3r2k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/6PP/3R2K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. The d8-rook is the sole guard. Find the win.
139	3. **FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/4RPPP/4R1K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Two rooks, one defender: force mate.
140	4. **FEN:** 2r3k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/5PPP/2R3K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Whose deflection works here? Evaluate.
141	
142	<details><summary>Show answers</summary>
143	
144	1. **1.Rd8#.** There is no back-rank defender at all, so the rook delivers immediate back-rank mate — the king on g8 is boxed by f7/g7/h7. (This is pure back-rank mate, *not* deflection: no defender exists to deflect.)
145	2. **1.Rxd8#.** The d8-rook is itself the only guard, so capturing it gives check, and the boxed king cannot recapture or flee. Note this is **removing the defender by capture**, the close cousin of deflection — here the defender is taken outright rather than driven away.
146	3. **1.Re8! Rxe8** (forced, else 2.Rxf8#) **2.Rxe8#.** The lead rook deflects the f8-rook off the back rank; the second rook recaptures with mate.
147	4. **Equal — no winning deflection.** Rooks on c8 and c1 with symmetric pawns: **1.Rxc8+ Rxc8** just trades into a drawn ending, and Black has the same resource. Neither side has a *second* attacker, so there is nothing to over-load. Correct evaluation: balanced.
148	
149	</details>
150	
151	## Related
152	
153	- [Decoy](decoy.md) — luring a piece *to* a square (the mirror image of deflection).
154	- [Removing the defender](removing-the-defender.md) — the parent family; capture instead of drive away.
155	- [Interference](interference.md) — block the defender's line instead of moving it.
156	- [Overloading](overloading.md) — the over-worked piece that deflection exploits.
157	- [Back-rank mate](back-rank-mate.md) — the most common target of deflection.
158	
159	Tags: [[deflection]] [[decoy]] [[removing-the-defender]] [[overloading]] [[back-rank-mate]]
160
